actions.thought: I am in the button position with 2s 8d, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Playing such a low-value hand out of position or even on the button in a multi-way pot is negative expected value. Given this is the first hand of a 150-hand tournament, I should prioritize discipline and conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o, which is a very weak hand. Even though the Button folded and I only have to call 50 chips to complete the blind, J4o is highly unprofitable to play out of position against the Big Blind. The best decision is to fold and move on to the next hand.

In 3-handed play, A9o on the Button is a strong hand that plays well in position. An open-raise applies pressure on the blinds and takes the betting lead. I'll open to 2.5x the big blind (250) to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot in position.

K2o is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind, especially when facing a raise from the Button. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand makes it difficult to realize equity. The pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) do not justify a call with such a low-equity hand that is easily dominated.

65o is a weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Even with decent pot odds, defending the big blind with this hand against a button open is generally suboptimal, and it is better to preserve my stack for better opportunities early in the tournament.
KQs is a premium hand in a short-handed tournament. Being on the button provides a significant positional advantage. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds.

K9o is a reasonable hand to defend from the small blind against a button open in a 3-handed game. Folding is too tight, and while 3-betting is an option, it is risky to do so with K9o out of position against a range that might include stronger broadway cards. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent holding while controlling the pot size.

Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, Q6o is far too weak of a hand to defend from the Big Blind. Even getting decent pot odds, the hand lacks playability and is often dominated, making it an easy fold.

The flop texture of Ace-high heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Holding only King-high with a backdoor flush draw, I have very little showdown value or significant equity. Playing out of position, checking is the prudent strategic choice to evaluate my opponent's aggression and avoid inflating a pot where I am likely currently behind.

I have flopped the nut flush draw along with a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway nuts. This is a massive amount of equity (roughly 45% against a made hand like an Ace). Since Flashlight checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of approximately 65% of the pot. This allows me to take the pot down immediately if they missed, or build a large pot for the instances where I hit one of my many outs on the turn or river. Being in position allows me to control the action on later streets.

The flop texture of Ace-High (Ah Th 6s) is highly favorable to the pre-flop aggressor's opening range. With only King-High and a weak backdoor flush draw, I have minimal showdown value and insufficient equity to justify a call against a continuation bet. My opponent is likely betting with a range that includes strong Aces or pocket pairs that have me beat. Folding here is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I am on the button with an Ace-high hand. This is a strong position to steal the blinds, especially in a 3-handed game. Raising to 250 is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to dictate the flow of the hand.

Holding AhKs in the small blind facing a button open, I have a premium hand. A 3-bet is mandatory for value and to take the initiative out of position. A sizing of 3.5x to 4x the open (around 900 chips) is standard to build the pot and discourage multi-way action.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit (Tc 9s). Flashlight (Button) opened to 250, and Pro (Small Blind) 3-bet to 900. T9o is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, particularly out of position against a range that likely includes strong broadways and pairs. Calling 800 more into a 1250 pot is not profitable given the poor playability of my hand post-flop. I will fold.

I opened the pot from the button with Ace-Deuce, and the small blind (Pro) 3-bet to 900. While I have position, A2s is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially one of this size. The opponent is representing a strong range, and calling would require committing 650 chips into a potentially difficult post-flop scenario where I am likely dominated by stronger Aces or pairs. Considering this is early in a 150-hand tournament, I should play conservatively and preserve my stack rather than engaging in a high-variance spot with a marginal holding.

Holding 4c 2s, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, and offsuit hand. Acting first pre-flop from the Button, the most profitable decision is to simply fold and wait for a better starting hand rather than trying to steal the blinds with bottom-tier cards.

I am in the Small Blind with 86 offsuit. Since the button folded, it is down to me and the Big Blind. While a raise or a call can be justified in a blind-vs-blind battle to steal the pot or see a cheap flop, 86o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. I have been active in previous hands, so folding here is a low-variance play that preserves my stack and balances my image.

I am on the button with Ten-Trey offsuit (Tc 3s). This is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button usually opens a wide range to steal the blinds, T3o is near the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop or against resistance. Given the stack depths and the fact that both opponents have shown some aggression in previous hands, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong hand heads-up. The button has already folded, so I am facing only the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard move to apply pressure and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand with the best starting range.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, Ace-Queen suited is a premium hand that plays very well in a 3-bet pot in position. I want to build the pot, extract value from worse Aces, Queens, and suited connectors, and take the betting initiative. A 3-bet to 900 puts maximum pressure on Flashlight's wider blind-vs-blind opening range.

Facing a 3-bet from the big blind while out of position with Ace-Nine offsuit is a difficult spot. A-9o does not play well against a strong 3-betting range, and continuing in a bloated pot without the initiative and out of position would likely lead to a negative EV outcome on later streets. I choose to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Queen-six suited. In a three-handed game, the button provides a significant positional advantage. Q6s is a playable hand in this position to open and attempt to steal the blinds. Raising to 250, which is 2.5 times the big blind, is a standard opening size to put pressure on the opponents while maintaining control of the pot.

I am dealt 2d 7s in the Small Blind, which is notoriously the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand, especially out of position. It's an easy fold.

Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong hand in 3-handed play against a Button opening range. Given that Flashlight has previously folded to 3-bets in hands #4 and #6, I will put in a 3-bet to 800 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. This capitalizes on Flashlight's recent tendency to fold to aggression while holding a hand that has good showdown value if called.

I opened Q6s on the button as a standard steal attempt. Flash (the big blind) responded with a 3-bet to 800. Q6s is a weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, as it is often dominated by the opponent's value range (like higher broadways or pairs). Calling 550 to play a bloated pot out of position against a range that likely has me beat is -EV. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the Button with KQ offsuit, a very strong hand for a 3-handed game. I want to open with a standard 2.5x raise to either steal the blinds or build the pot while having positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o. The Button has opened for 2.5x. While K9o is a somewhat decent hand, it plays poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive Button who has shown several raises in previous hands. Calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or join the pot, making the hand even harder to play. Folding is the most disciplined choice here to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position.

Facing a 2.5x open from the button, my 9c 8h is a standard defense in the big blind. The pot odds are favorable, allowing me to call the additional 150 to see a flop against a wide button opening range.

The flop is extremely dry and heavily favors the button's opening range, as an Ace and Queen are both high-card features that connect well with a standard button raising range. My 9-high hand has no equity (no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw). There is no value in betting here, and I should check to keep the pot small or prepare to fold to any significant aggression from my opponent.

With middle pair and a King kicker on an Ace-high flop, I have decent showdown value but am likely behind any Ace that the Big Blind might have defended with. There are also spade flush draws possible. Checking back provides pot control, keeps the pot small with a marginal made hand, and allows me to see the turn card for free.

I have 9-high with absolutely no equity (no pair, no draws) on a board of As-Qh-2s-7d. The opponent checked the flop behind me, which could indicate a weak hand, but there is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff here. Betting would force me to commit chips that I am unlikely to get back, especially since I have no way to improve on the river besides hitting a nine or eight, which is very unlikely to be the best hand. Checking is the prudent choice to keep the pot small and minimize potential losses.

Flashlight has checked both the flop and the turn, showing significant weakness and likely not holding an Ace. I have second pair with a premium kicker (KQ). Betting now allows me to extract value from worse hands like weaker Queens, smaller pocket pairs, and various draws (like flush or straight draws) while simultaneously protecting my equity from those draws hitting on the river. A half-pot bet is a standard sizing to achieve both value and protection.

The opponent checked the flop, which could have indicated a weak hand or a slow-play, but their turn bet indicates they have now connected with the board or are betting for value. With nine-high and zero equity (no pair, no draws), my hand is effectively worthless in this spot. Calling 275 to see a river is mathematically incorrect as I have essentially zero chance of winning a showdown against the opponent's range on an Ace-Queen high board. Therefore, the only logical action is to fold.

Holding Ace-Jack suited (AsJs) on the button in a three-handed game is a very strong hand. It is a mandatory opening raise to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard size that allows me to take control of the betting lead while maintaining positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

Holding King-Four suited in the small blind against a button raise is a clear fold. I am out of position for the rest of the hand, and my hand strength is insufficient to justify calling or re-raising against a tight or aggressive opener. Folding preserves my stack and avoids playing a speculative hand from a disadvantageous position.
Opponent on the Button raised to 3BB. I have 62 suited in the Big Blind. This hand is far too weak to defend, even closing the action. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am in the button position in a 3-handed game. With King-Trey suited (Kd 3d), I have a hand with enough equity to open the action. Given that my opponents have been playing aggressively, raising allows me to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or dictate the pace of the hand. A raise to 250 chips is a standard opening size in this spot.

Pocket deuces are the lowest pair and difficult to play out of position from the small blind. Calling here invites a potential squeeze from the big blind, and even if we see a flop, it will be hard to realize our equity without flopping a set. Given the positional disadvantage, folding is the safer and more standard play to conserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 2.5x the big blind (250). With 100 already in the pot, I only need to call 150 more to see a flop. The pot odds are 400:150, or roughly 2.67 to 1, which is more than enough to justify a defense with a hand as strong as K9o against a likely wide Button opening range. Flashlight has shown a tendency to raise and then fold to 3-bets in previous hands, but K9o functions well as a calling hand here rather than a 3-bet bluff. I will call to see the flop and play from out of position.

I defended the big blind with K9o pre-flop against a 2.5x button open. The flop of Qc 7s 4s does not hit my range well, and I currently only have King-high. I do have the King of Spades for a backdoor flush draw and one overcard to the Queen, but I should not be leading into the pre-flop aggressor here. Checking is the standard play to see how Flashlight proceeds with their positional advantage.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on the button. The flop of Queen-Seven-Four (rainbow) is relatively dry and likely missed both of our ranges, although it hits a Queen-range slightly better. With King-high and a backdoor flush draw, checking is a viable option to keep the pot small, but a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (275 chips) is a standard aggressive play to test the opponent's strength and potentially take down the pot now given my positional advantage.

I defended the big blind with K9o and missed the flop completely. The board is Qc 7s 4s, and Flashlight has placed a half-pot continuation bet. While I have the King of Spades for a backdoor flush draw and one overcard, King-high is too weak to continue out of position against a range that contains many Queens and better made hands. I am getting 3:1 odds, but my actual equity is likely lower than 25% given the lack of direct draws. Folding is the correct move here to preserve my stack.

Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even from the Button in a 3-handed game. There is no need to commit chips with such a marginal holding when we have a comfortable stack and plenty of time left in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with J9s, a strong hand for blind-vs-blind play. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind, as J9s has good playability and equity. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-7 offsuit, which is a marginal hand with low playability. Flash has raised to 3x from the Small Blind. Given the early stage of the tournament and the weak strength of my starting hand, calling would likely lead to a difficult post-flop scenario out of position. It is best to fold and conserve my stack.

A8o is a strong enough hand to open from the button in 3-handed play. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position. I'll stick with my previous sizing of 3x (300) to keep my range consistent and apply pressure.

K3s is a weak hand to defend out of position from the small blind against a button raise. Calling would put me in a disadvantageous spot post-flop without initiative. It is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Big Blind with A4 offsuit. With no antes, the pot odds are not particularly compelling to defend such a marginal, reverse-implied-odds type of hand out of position. A 3-bet bluff could be considered with a suited Ace, but offsuit is too weak to play optimally against a Button opening range. Folding is the safest and most standard choice.

I am on the button with 73o, which is an extremely weak hand with no real playability. Even in a 3-handed game where opening ranges are wide, this hand does not have enough equity to justify entering the pot. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

With pocket sixes in the small blind and the button folded, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise. A raise to 3 BBs (300) pressures the big blind and often takes down the pot immediately, while still playing fine if called.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8c 5h. The Button has folded and the Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While I would have position on Pro post-flop, 85o is an extremely weak hand that doesn't play well even with the benefit of position. It is well outside the standard defending range against a 3x raise. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

KQo on the button is a strong starting hand that plays well in position. A standard open-raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) will thin the field, take control of the hand, and often win the blinds outright.

I hold King-Ace offsuit (Ks Ah) in the Small Blind. This is a premium starting hand. The Button (Pro) has opened with a raise to 250. To capitalize on the strength of my hand and to protect against the Big Blind entering the pot or being forced to play a large pot out of position without the initiative, I will 3-bet. A standard 3-bet size from the blinds is typically 3.5x to 4x the open raise. Based on my previous sizing and Pro's aggressive history, a raise to 900 is appropriate to build the pot and potentially end the hand immediately.

I hold 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a raise from the Button and a strong 3-bet from the Small Blind, there is absolutely no incentive to continue with this hand. Playing out of position against two aggressive opponents with such a poor range would be a significant strategic error. Folding is the only correct decision to preserve my stack for a more profitable opportunity.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after opening on the Button. KQo is a solid hand to open, but it performs poorly against a 3-betting range which consists of hands that dominate it like AK, AQ, AA, KK, QQ. While we have position, defending KQo to a sizable 3-bet is unprofitable. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.

I have pocket Kings, which is an extremely strong starting hand. My goal is to build the pot and extract value from my opponents. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to narrow the field and maximize profit.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. Since Flash has opened on the button, I want to take the initiative and potentially isolate him, as calling out of position would be difficult to play post-flop. A 3-bet to 950 puts pressure on the button and allows me to dictate the flow of the hand.

Facing a button open and a strong 3-bet from the small blind, our A8 suited is too weak to call or 4-bet. The small blind's 3-betting range is likely very strong, dominating our hand, and the original raiser is still to act behind us. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

I have pocket Kings, the second-strongest starting hand. Flashlight's 3-bet from the small blind indicates a strong range, but KK is well ahead of most of his continuing range. I will 4-bet to 2400 to build the pot, gain initiative, and potentially get commitment from hands like QQ, JJ, or AK.

After I 3-bet to 950, Flash 4-bet to 2400. My Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand in a 3-handed environment, and the pot odds (calling 1450 to win a total pot of 4900) are favorable to continue. While I will be out of position post-flop, A-Jo has enough equity against a wide button 4-bet range to justify a call. Folding would be too conservative, and an all-in 5-bet would be too high variance against his range.

I am in a 4-bet pot out of position. The flop of Ts-7d-3d is relatively dry and misses my Ace-Jack offsuit completely, aside from a backdoor flush draw. Given that Flash initiated a 4-bet pre-flop, their range is heavily weighted toward high pairs and AK/AQ, meaning they likely have me beat with any pair or even higher Ace-high hands. Betting into a 4-bet range as a bluff is high variance and unlikely to succeed given the strength they've shown. Checking is the most prudent path to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on their action.

I have an overpair (Kings) on a relatively safe board of Ts-7d-3d. The SPR is low (around 1.25), and Flashlight's check indicates he is likely in check-call mode with hands like JJ, QQ, or perhaps a flush draw. I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against an Ace or a diamond on the turn. A bet of around 1/3 to 40% of the pot is appropriate here to keep his range wide while building the pot for a potential shove on later streets.

I am facing a continuation bet in a 4-bet pot on a board of Ts-7d-3d. The pot is 6650 and the bet is 1750, meaning I am getting pot odds of roughly 21% (1750 to win a total pot of 8400). While my Ace-Jack offsuit has missed the board, I have backdoor flush potential and two overcards against a range that may include smaller pairs or missed broadway combinations. Given the pot odds and the fact that I only have 3800 remaining in my stack, folding here would be too exploitable and conservative. Calling allows me to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn, even though I will be effectively pot-committed.

With top pair on a relatively dry board after calling a 4-bet and a flop continuation bet, my hand has improved significantly. However, being out of position with a short stack in a 4-bet pot, I need to proceed cautiously. Checking is the optimal play here to induce a bet from the opponent, as I am likely ahead of his bluffs (like broadway hands) and can re-evaluate on the river. Leading out would effectively commit my remaining chips into a potentially strong range.

The Ace of Hearts is a significant scare card for my pocket Kings. Flashlight called a 4-bet pre-flop and a flop bet, suggesting a strong range that includes AK, QQ, JJ, and potentially diamond draws. Since the Ace is more likely to be in a 4-bet caller's range (like AK) or could have been the card I was representing, the board texture has shifted. With an SPR of roughly 0.5, I am effectively committed if I bet, but checking back allows me to control the pot size, see the river for free, and potentially induce a bluff from lower pairs like QQ or JJ on the river. It also prevents me from value-owning myself against an Ace that won't fold.

The river 5d completed a potential flush draw on the board (Ts-7d-3d-Ah-5d). With Ace-Jack (Top Pair, Ace high), I have a marginal showdown hand that is vulnerable to flushes and two-pairs. My opponent checked the turn, signaling potential weakness or a medium-strength hand that they were checking for control. Given the pot size and my remaining stack, checking the river is the optimal play to reach showdown affordably and avoid unnecessary risk, as betting would likely only get called by better hands or fold out worse ones.

The board has run out with an Ace and three diamonds, both of which are unfavorable for my pocket Kings. Flashlight called a 4-bet pre-flop and check-called the flop, which puts a significant number of Aces (like AK or AQs) and potentially diamond draws in his range. By checking the turn and now the river, Flashlight is either showing weakness with hands like JJ or QQ, or he is playing a marginal Ace or a flush cautiously. Given the pot size and the board texture, there is very little value in betting as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I risk being check-raised or called by hands that have me beaten. Checking back ensures I reach showdown and realize my equity.

Kc6c is a suited hand with a king, making it a viable candidate for a steal attempt on the button in a 3-handed game. Opening to 300 is a standard size to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

73s is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the pre-flop raiser. The hand lacks the raw equity and playability needed to justify a call, and a 3-bet bluff with this holding is unnecessary. Folding is the clear optimal play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s7s. Flashlight, the button, has raised to 300. The Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of 2.25:1 (approx 31%). Suited connectors and one-gappers like 57s are generally strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a button open, especially with deep stacks. Although I will be out of position, the price is too good to fold, and the hand has good post-flop playability if I hit a straight, flush draw, or two pair.

On this Ace-high board (As 3h Jh), I have missed completely, holding only a backdoor spade flush draw with 5s7s. As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Flashlight, the button raiser, is likely to have many Aces and Jacks in their range, making this board unfavorable for a lead. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

The board texture is Ace-high with a Jack, which is favorable for the Big Blind's calling range. Checking back is the most prudent play here to maintain pot control and realize equity with King-high. Betting into an Ace-high board with K-high is likely to be called by any Ace or Jack, making it an ineffective bluffing spot.

On the turn, the board is As 3h Jh 2h. I have 7s 5s, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Flashlight checked back on the flop, which could indicate a weak range or a trap. Since I have almost no showdown value and am out of position, checking is the safest play. If Flashlight checks behind again, I get a free look at the river to potentially hit my gutshot. If Flashlight bets, I will likely fold unless the price is exceptionally cheap, as my hand is currently just 7-high.

With K-high and no heart in my hand on a board that now has three hearts (As-3h-Jh-2h), I have very little equity to improve and my showdown value is minimal. The Big Blind checked to me, which could indicate a trap or a weak hand that missed the board. Betting here into an Ace-high board with 3 hearts is unlikely to fold out any hand that has equity against me, as an Ace or a Jack will likely call. Checking back is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and take a free look at the river.

The board is As 3h Jh 2h Jd and I hold 7-high. Flashlight raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, suggesting a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to bet, or a hand that has completely missed. While I have no showdown value, bluffing on this board is risky because Flashlight's checks often indicate a willingness to reach showdown with a mediocre hand (like a small pair or a weak Ace). Since I am out of position and the pot is relatively small, I will check and concede the hand rather than risking a bluff against a range that likely beats 7-high and might not fold.

I hold King-high on a board of As-Jh-2h-3h-Jd. My opponent has checked to me on the river. Given the board texture and the action, any pair (Ace, Jack, 3, or 2) beats me. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands, as it is difficult to represent a hand that beats their calling range effectively on this board. Since I have zero showdown value against their pair range and no clear path to bluff them off a better hand, checking behind is the most prudent play to avoid losing additional chips.

Queen-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand. While it can sometimes be opened from the button in a 3-handed game, the offsuit nature makes it vulnerable to 3-bets from the aggressive blinds. With over 100 big blinds, I can afford to play slightly tighter and wait for a more premium holding to attack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine suited (Ac 9c), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, especially when it is folded to me. Standard strategy in a blind-versus-blind situation is to raise for value and to put pressure on the Big Blind. A raise to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce suited, which is a very weak hand. The Small Blind (Flash) has raised to 300, and calling would require committing an additional 200 chips into the pot. Given the strength of the hand and the position, there is no profitable reason to defend. Folding is the standard and correct mathematical play.

On the button in a three-handed game, King-Ten offsuit (KTo) is a very strong hand that should be opened 100% of the time. My stack of 65.5 big blinds gives me plenty of room to play post-flop, and a standard 3x open (300 chips) has been effective in previous hands to either take down the blinds or define ranges.

My hand (3h-4s) is extremely weak and offers poor playability. Being in the small blind, I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against the button, which puts me at a significant disadvantage. There is no strategic value in defending this hand against a raise, so folding is the correct and disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

Holding 6h 9d offsuit in the Big Blind is a very weak starting hand. The Button has opened to 3x the big blind, and playing this hand out of position will be unprofitable. The clear decision is to fold.

7-10 offsuit is a weak, non-connected, and non-suited hand with very poor playability. Even on the button, there is no strategic justification to enter the pot with this holding. Conserving chips for a better opportunity is the most disciplined play.

I am in the Small Blind with T5o. The Button folded, so it's a blind vs. blind situation. However, T5o is an extremely weak hand and playing it out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding is the best choice.
Holding Q3o on the button. This is a very weak hand with no suitedness or connectivity, making it unprofitable to play in most situations. Folding is the standard and best decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h 6s, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Flashlight has a significant chip lead. Completing or raising with 2-6 offsuit is generally unprofitable, especially out of position. I will fold to preserve my stack for better starting hands.

I am on the Button with Ace-Eight suited, a premium hand in 3-handed play. Standard opening strategy dictates a raise here to put pressure on the blinds and gain control of the pot. I've consistently used a 3x sizing for my opens in previous hands, and I will maintain that consistency here.

K-5 offsuit is a weak hand to defend with, especially from the small blind while being out of position against a button raise. The pot odds do not warrant a call, and folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button with a weak suited hand like Q3s in the Big Blind. Calling is generally negative EV, and 3-betting as a bluff with this specific hand is not standard. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

Seven-four suited is a very weak starting hand, even when in position on the button. There is no significant advantage to playing this hand against two opponents given the current blind structure. It is better to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable hand with better equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 5h, which is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Even though it is only 50 more chips to call, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding is the best decision to preserve my stack.

On the button with A4o, we have a weak offsuit Ace, but it is strong enough to attempt a steal against the blinds in a 3-handed game. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the small and big blinds to fold their marginal holdings.

In this 3-handed situation, I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Eight suited. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard 2.5x raise. While Q8s has some potential in terms of flushes and straights, playing it out of position against the original raiser and with the Big Blind (Flashlight) still to act is disadvantageous. Given that I am currently the shortest stack of the three, I prefer to fold and wait for a more profitable spot rather than calling and being sandwiched out of position for the rest of the hand.

Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong enough holding to defend the big blind against a button open, especially given the favorable pot odds. I have invested 100 chips as the big blind, and calling 150 more to see a flop is a standard, disciplined play to maintain pot control and evaluate post-flop.

The flop is 7d-2s-9h, and I have middle pair with my 9c. As the out-of-position player who defended the big blind, the standard and most strategic play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to gauge their interest in the pot through their action before deciding whether to call or fold, keeping the pot manageable with a marginal made hand.

The flop is relatively dry and disconnected (7-2-9). As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the betting initiative. While my Ace-high might occasionally be the best hand here, it is vulnerable to various overcards and straight draws. A standard continuation bet of half the pot puts pressure on the big blind's marginal defending range and will often win the pot uncontested by folding out hands like JT, QT, or KJ. I will bet 275.

I have middle pair with a 9, which is a solid bluff-catcher on this dry, rainbow board (7-2-9). The opponent (Pro) could be continuation betting with overcards or air. Calling 275 is the standard play here to keep the pot manageable, allow the opponent to continue bluffing on future streets, and maintain pot control with a marginal holding.

I am out of position with a pair of 9s on a 7-2-9-2 board. The turn card, the 2d, is relatively blank and pairs the board. My hand is a decent bluff-catcher, but it is not strong enough to lead out for value. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate any action from the opponent (Pro) on the turn. If Pro bets, I can decide whether to continue based on the sizing, but leading out would only inflate the pot with a marginal hand.

After the big blind check-calls our flop continuation bet, the turn brings a 2, pairing the bottom card of the flop and introducing a diamond draw. Our Ace-high has some showdown value against missed straight draws (like JT, T8, J8) but is behind any made pair (7x, 9x, or pocket pairs). Continuing to bluff here would require firing a large bet to fold out those pairs, which is an unnecessary risk. By checking behind, we control the size of the pot, realize our equity, and can potentially win at showdown or hit an Ace on the river.

On the river, the board pairs the Ace, giving me two pair (Aces and Nines), which is a very strong hand in this spot. The opponent checked the turn, signaling weakness, and has not yet bet on the river. I should now shift from a defensive posture to a value-betting strategy to extract chips from any weaker Aces or middle pairs the opponent might be holding. Betting approximately half-pot (600) is a standard value size to encourage a call while remaining disciplined.

On the river, we improve to top pair with our Ace. Flashlight leads out for 600 into a pot of 1100. Since we checked back the turn, Flashlight might interpret our check as weakness and attempt to bluff a missed straight draw (like JT, T8) or a float that missed. While our kicker (4) is weak, top pair is a very strong bluff-catcher in this spot. We are getting good pot odds to call, and our hand beats all of his bluffs. Raising would only get called by better hands (like two pair, a better Ace, or trips), so a flat call is the optimal decision.

I am on the button with an Ace in a three-handed game. Ace-rag is a standard opening hand from this position to apply pressure on the blinds. My previous 3x raises have been effective in picking up the blinds, so I will continue with that sizing.

I am in the small blind with 8h-7s offsuit, which is a weak hand to defend out of position. The opener (Flash on the button) has a range advantage, and calling would put me in a difficult spot for the remainder of the hand. Folding is the disciplined play to avoid bleeding chips.

I hold AQs in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x open from the Button. Flash has been opening very frequently, so his range is quite wide. AQs is a premium hand that plays very well as a 3-bet for value and to take the betting lead out of position. I'll 3-bet to 1000 to build the pot and extract value.

I opened from the button with Ah 4d, which is a standard steal attempt in a three-handed game. Pro has 3-bet me to 1000 from the Big Blind. While I have position, A4o is a weak hand that doesn't play well post-flop against a 3-betting range. Since Pro has likely noticed my frequent opening raises and is starting to defend aggressively, I will fold this marginal hand and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Holding 5s 2d, one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With no equity and being in an early position to act, the only rational decision is to fold and preserve my chip stack for a better opportunity.

Action folds to me in the Small Blind with A6 suited. This is a strong starting hand in a blind vs. blind scenario that plays well post-flop, but taking down the pot right now is also a good result. Raising to 3x (300 chips) puts pressure on the Big Blind to defend with sub-optimal hands.

I am in the Big Blind with A9o, which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind, a standard play that could represent a wide range of hands. In previous hands, Pro has folded to 3-bets (Hand #14), and I want to exert pressure to either take the pot down now or build it while I likely have the best hand and position for the rest of the hand. A raise to 900 is a standard 3-bet sizing that puts the decision back on Pro.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after opening from the Small Blind, A6 suited is too weak to call out of position. While it has flush potential and an Ace blocker, playing it post-flop against a strong 3-bet range often leads to being dominated when an Ace falls. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Holding Qs 2s on the Button is too weak of a hand to open, despite being suited. The gap between the Queen and the Deuce makes it a poor candidate for stealing the blinds, so the standard play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. It's folded to me. While blind-vs-blind situations allow for wider ranges, Ten-Seven offsuit is generally too weak to open-raise or complete profitably, especially against a deep-stacked opponent in the Big Blind. I have been active in previous hands, so folding here preserves my stack and avoids playing a weak hand out of position.

I am on the button with 8h 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have the positional advantage, the hand strength is too low to justify an open, especially considering that the Big Blind (Pro) has shown a tendency to 3-bet. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

7-5 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when playing out of position against the Big Blind. Since there is no reason to risk more chips with such a marginal holding, folding is the most disciplined and prudent strategic choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding pocket aces, the strongest starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Being on the button, I have the positional advantage. I want to build the pot and isolate an opponent, so a standard raise to 300 is appropriate to start the action aggressively.

K8 suited is a marginal hand when facing a 3x open from the Button. Being in the Small Blind means we will be out of position for the rest of the hand, and calling risks the Big Blind squeezing behind us. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but folding is the safer, standard play here to conserve chips.

I have a strong pocket pair (88) in the Big Blind against a button raise from Flashlight. In a 3-handed game, pocket eights are well ahead of a typical button opening range. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, 3-betting for value and to take control of the pot is standard here. I'll raise to 950, which is a little over 3x the initial raise, to extract value and potentially end the hand immediately.

I hold pocket Aces, the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The opponent (Flash) has 3-bet my opening raise, which indicates they have a strong hand or are trying to take the pot down. Given my position and the strength of my hand, I should definitely not fold. Simply calling would allow the opponent to realize their equity cheaply and might keep me guessing on a coordinated board. I will 4-bet to build the pot and extract value. A raise to 2,400 puts significant pressure on the opponent while remaining in a position to stack them if they decide to shove or call.

Flashlight has 4-bet to 2400 after my 3-bet to 950. While 88 is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play, a 4-bet usually represents a very narrow and strong range (typically TT+, AK, and occasionally AQs). My current stack of 5850 means I would be committing nearly half my stack just to call, leaving me in a difficult position out of position on most flops. If I jam, I am likely only called by hands that dominate me or at best I'm in a coin flip against AK/AQ. Given the large size of the 4-bet and the fact that we are only at hand 28 of 150, I prefer to fold and preserve my remaining 4900 chips rather than risk my tournament life or a significant portion of my stack on a marginal hand.

K7o is a very marginal holding even from the button. Against opponents who have shown aggression and given the risk of being 3-bet or playing a weak top pair post-flop out of position if called by the blinds, the most disciplined and standard play is to fold and look for a better spot to steal or play a pot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation after the Button has folded. I want to raise to build the pot and potentially take the blinds immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Defending my big blind with Jc 8c against a small blind open is mathematically sound. With 200 chips to call and a pot of 400, I have sufficient pot odds, and the hand plays well post-flop given our effective stacks.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a heads-up pot against the Big Blind. The board is Qs-Qd-6s, which is a favorable texture for my range. I hold Ace-high (AdJs) with a backdoor flush draw. I will lead with a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to put pressure on the opponent's weaker holdings and represent a strong hand like a Queen, an overpair, or a high Ace.

The flop of [6s, Qs, Qd] has left me with [8c, Jc], which is essentially air with no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. While the opponent's bet of 300 into a 600 pot offers 3:1 pot odds, I have virtually zero equity against a range that includes a Queen, pocket pairs, or better high-card hands. Calling here would be a 'hopeful' call without mathematical justification, and continuing to the turn will likely lead to further bets I cannot call. It is best to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable board.

I am on the button with Queen-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. With a stack of 6,150 chips, I am currently the shortest stack at the table. While a button open is standard with a wide range, Q2o is near the bottom of that range and doesn't play well post-flop if called. Given the aggressive nature of the blinds (Pro and Flashlight have shown willingness to 3-bet and defend), I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

I am in the small blind with 6d7d after the button folded. With a deep stack and a suited connector, completing the blind (calling the additional 50) is a standard and effective strategy to see a flop cheaply in a heads-up situation against the big blind.

With K7o in the big blind facing a limp from the small blind, my hand is marginal. I have the option to check and see a free flop in position. Raising could fold out some weak hands, but K7o doesn't have enough equity to build a big pot right now. Taking a free flop is the standard and safest play.

I am in the small blind and checked into the big blind pre-flop. The flop comes K-A-5 rainbow, which is an extremely strong board for the big blind's range, as they will have a significant amount of Kx and Ax hands in their check-back range. With 7-high and no draw, I have zero equity and cannot represent any strength. Checking is the only sensible move to control the size of the pot and prepare to fold to any aggression.

I flopped middle pair (Kings) on a dry board with an Ace overcard. Flashlight limped pre-flop and checked the flop, indicating weakness. However, betting here might only get called by better hands like an Ace or a better King, while folding out worse hands. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my showdown value, and evaluate the turn card, keeping his bluffs and weaker holdings in his range.

I am in the small blind with a pair of 7s on a board of K-A-5-7. While I improved to a pair on the turn, it is a very weak pair on this broadway-heavy board. Checking is the standard play here as betting would only isolate me against better hands or get called by hands that have me beat. I want to keep the pot small and look to get to showdown or fold to any significant aggression from the big blind.

The turn gives me two pair (Kings and Sevens), which is a very strong hand given the pre-flop limp and the double check from my opponent. Since Flashlight has checked twice, it's unlikely he has a strong Ace. I should bet for value to get called by worse Kings, mid-pairs, or any flush/straight draws that have picked up equity on the turn. A bet of around 75% of the pot sizes it well to charge draws and build the pot for the river.

The opponent checked the flop, which indicates they likely do not have a strong Ace or King, or they are playing a passive line. By hitting a pair of 7s on the turn, I have improved my equity slightly against their potential bluffs or small pairs (like 5x). Getting 2.3-to-1 pot odds, calling is the standard play to keep the pot controlled and see the river, as folding a pair to a small bet seems too conservative, and raising provides no benefit against their range.

I am in the small blind and first to act on the river. The board is Kh-Ac-5d-7h-9s, which is a very dry, broadway-heavy board. My pair of 7s is extremely weak and unlikely to be the best hand, as the opponent's range, even after checking the flop, is heavily weighted towards Aces, Kings, and middle pairs that have me crushed. Betting would only isolate me against better hands, and checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown, or fold if the opponent makes a significant bet.

The river brings the 9s, missing the turn heart draw. My Kings and Sevens two pair is well-disguised and beats any single pair of Aces or Kings that Flashlight might have check-called with on the turn. While some better two-pair combinations like A5, A7, A9, or K9 beat me, there are far more combinations of single-pair hands in his limping range, such as a weak Ace (A2-A8, AT) or a worse King. I should make a value bet here because opponents frequently call with top pair. A bet of around 60% of the pot is perfectly sized to extract maximum value from these marginal holdings.

My pair of 7s is extremely weak on this broadway-heavy board. The opponent checked the flop, which could indicate a slow-played Ace or King, or simply a mediocre hand. However, their subsequent bets on the turn and river show they have found value or are making a reasonable attempt to win the pot. Given the strength of the board and my lack of equity against any Ace, King, or even 8s/9s, calling 300 to potentially lose is not a profitable long-term decision. Folding is the most prudent action.

9-7 offsuit is a weak starting hand to play from the button, even in a short-handed game. With a substantial stack of over 140 big blinds, I do not need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. Folding is the most disciplined play here to conserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.
T4 suited is a very weak hand from the small blind facing the big blind. Without position or a strong range advantage, folding is the standard and safest play to conserve chips.

I am first to act on the Button with 54o, which is a weak unsuited connector. In a 3-handed game, this hand lacks the equity, high-card value, and playability to profitably open-raise. A standard fold is the best choice here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 5c, a very weak starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While I could attempt to steal the blinds, Flashlight (the Big Blind) has a significant chip lead and has shown willingness to defend or re-raise in previous hands. Completing the small blind with such a low-equity hand puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I have been dealt Jack of Diamonds and Jack of Clubs on the button. This is a premium pocket pair, especially in a three-handed game. My stack is currently 6150, which is around 61 big blinds. The standard play here is to raise for value and to gain control of the pot. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing that allows me to build the pot while still having flexibility if re-raised. Given the action in previous hands, Flashlight and Pro have shown some aggression, so I want to present a strong range while holding a hand that plays well post-flop or can comfortably continue against most three-bets.

Holding 63 suited is a weak hand that lacks high card strength and playability against a button open. Being out of position makes playing this hand even more difficult. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, King-Four suited is too weak to defend profitably out of position. The pot odds aren't quite good enough to justify a call, and a 3-bet bluff with this hand is unnecessary. Folding is the most solid and standard play here.

I am dealt 9d 3s, a very weak hand with extremely low playability. Even on the button, there is no strategic justification to enter the pot with this holding against two opponents. I will fold and conserve my stack for a more profitable situation.

With 89 offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Stealing could work, but folding is the safer, standard play to conserve my stack and avoid sticky post-flop situations out of position against the Big Blind.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, it is generally a fold in a 3-handed game to avoid marginal situations and reverse implied odds, especially when facing blinds that might defend aggressively.

I am in the Small Blind with K5s. The Button (Pro) has folded. It is now just me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). K5s is a strong enough hand to play in a blind-vs-blind situation. While I could call the 50 to complete, raising is generally more effective to put pressure on the Big Blind and win the pot immediately. Looking at Flashlight's previous play, they have called raises from the BB before but also folded to aggression. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to define the hand or take it down now.

I am in the big blind with K-7 offsuit. While this is a weak hand, I am getting 2:1 pot odds to make the call (200 to win a total pot of 400). Given my stack size and the aggressive opening frequency of the Small Blind (Flash), defending the big blind here is mathematically reasonable to see a flop, even though the hand strength is marginal.

On a very dry, paired board (4-4-2), I have a range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. King-high with a backdoor flush draw is a decent hand to continuation bet. A half-pot bet (300) should be enough to fold out many of the Big Blind's non-pairing hands like overcards or weak draws, while also keeping the pot manageable if they have a piece of the board.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high (Ks-7h) on a very dry, paired board (4c-4h-2s). Flash (Small Blind) has bet 300 into a 600 pot. My hand has virtually no equity against a range that contains any 4, any pocket pair, or even Ace-high/medium-high cards which likely dominate my King-high. There is no strategic value in calling here as I am essentially drawing dead against any made hand and will likely be forced to fold on the turn or river anyway. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. My typical raise size in this tournament has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency to keep my range unprotected. While Ace-Five offsuit is not a premium hand, the 'Ace' blocker is valuable, and playing in position against Flashlight and Pro is advantageous.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Ten suited (Qc Ts), which is a reasonable hand to play against the button's opening raise. Given my large stack size compared to my opponents, I can afford to see a flop. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds offered (calling 250 to win a pot of 450) are acceptable for this holding. I will call to see if I can hit a strong pair or straight draw on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with an extremely weak hand, 8c 4c (a suited three-gapper). Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, my hand is vastly outclassed and plays terribly out of position in a multiway pot. Even with pot odds, this is a clear fold.

The flop is paired, which makes it less likely my opponent connected strongly unless they have a Jack or a pair of 2s. I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) but no pair and no strong draw other than a backdoor flush draw. As the out-of-position player who called pre-flop, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the most standard and safe play, allowing me to re-evaluate based on their action.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a board of 2s-2c-Jc. While I only have Ace-high, the board is relatively dry and favors a continuation bet. Flashlight checked, indicating weakness or a pot-controlling middle pair/Jack. I have the Ace of Clubs, which gives me a backdoor nut flush draw. A standard C-bet here of approximately half the pot should fold out most of Flashlight's range that missed the flop (small pairs, suited connectors) while allowing me to maintain initiative. If called, I have overcard outs and potential for the nut flush draw.

The opponent has made a continuation bet on a paired, dry board. My hand (Qc Ts) has two overcards to the Jack and a backdoor flush draw. Given the small bet size (300 into a 700 pot), the pot odds are attractive (approx. 3.33:1). Folding here is too conservative, and raising into a potentially strong range on a paired board is inefficient. Calling is the most balanced approach to see the turn and re-evaluate.

I am acting first on the turn, out of position in the small blind. The board is 2s 2c Jc 8h, which is paired and relatively dry. With only Queen-high, I have very little showdown value and no strong draw to justify betting. Checking is the optimal strategy here to control the pot size and act based on the opponent's subsequent decision.

On this turn card, the 8h is relatively blank. My opponent, Flashlight, has check-called the flop, which suggests they likely have a Jack, a pocket pair, or perhaps a drawing hand. With my Ace-high, I have some showdown value but limited equity against a made hand like Jx or a pocket pair. Betting again (a second barrel) is risky because Flashlight's range is quite sticky after calling the flop on such a dry board. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and can potentially induce a bluff or see a cheap showdown on the river. My Ace-high might still be the best hand if they were floating with a weaker high card or a draw.

I have improved to a pair of Queens on the river. The turn checked through, which suggests my opponent likely lacks a strong Jack or better, as they would have typically bet for value on the turn. Since I have top pair and the board is relatively dry, I believe I have the best hand frequently enough to go for a thin value bet. A bet of approximately one-third of the pot (400) should extract value from second-best hands like a Jack or an Eight while keeping the pot size manageable if I am raised.

On this board of 2s-2c-Jc-8h-Qh, I am holding Ace-high. Flashlight check-called my continuation bet on the flop, which suggests they have a piece of the board, likely a Jack, a pocket pair, or a flush draw. After we both checked the turn, the river brought a Queen. Flashlight's lead for 400 into a 1300 pot is a small value bet or a blocking bet. While the odds offered (400 to win 1700) are decent, Ace-high rarely wins here when an opponent leads into the pre-flop aggressor after a checked turn. Most of the hands Flashlight would call the flop with (Jx, 33-TT) now beat me, and some (like KQ or QJ) improved on the river. Without a strong read that Flashlight is over-bluffing missed club draws, folding is the most disciplined play.

I am on the button with King-Queen suited, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Opening with a raise is the standard play here to maximize my positional advantage and either take down the blinds or build a pot while in position. I will size the raise to 300, consistent with my previous betting patterns.

Flashlight opens frequently from the Button and has shown a tendency to fold to 3-bets in the past. K9s is a solid candidate to 3-bet semi-bluff from the small blind because it blocks strong hands like AK, KQ, and KK, and retains decent equity and playability if called. By raising to 1000, I put pressure on the Button to fold marginal opening hands and I prevent the Big Blind from overcalling or squeezing.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket fives. Flashlight opened from the button for 300, and Pro 3-bet from the small blind to 1000. While pocket pairs have set-mining value, a 3-bet of this size represents a strong range (likely 99+, AJ+). Calling 900 more would commit nearly 17% of my stack out of position, and the implied odds are not ideal given the stack-to-pot ratio if I don't hit a set. Additionally, Flashlight still has the option to 4-bet or call, creating a multi-way pot where small pairs perform poorly. To preserve my stack for better spots, folding is the most prudent play here.

King-Queen suited is a premium hand in a 3-handed game, and being on the button gives me a significant positional advantage. Against a 3-bet from the small blind, calling is the most balanced approach. A 4-bet would commit a large portion of the stack and might isolate me against stronger hands like Aces or Kings, whereas calling allows me to control the pot size and play the hand in position post-flop, where I can more easily navigate varying board textures.

The flop texture 7d 8c Qc heavily favors the caller's range, which includes medium pocket pairs, Qx hands, and various straight or flush draws. My Kh9h missed completely, leaving me with no made hand and virtually zero equity. C-betting here as a pure bluff is highly unprofitable because we will get called or raised very often. Checking to pot-control and preparing to fold to a bet is the safest and most optimal play.

I have top pair with King-Queen on a board of 7d-8c-Qc. The opponent (Pro) checked after 3-betting pre-flop, which could indicate a trap or a desire to play a check-call line. Betting here could alienate weaker hands while isolating myself against better holdings (like pocket Aces or Kings) or a slow-played set. Checking behind allows me to maintain pot control, realize my hand's equity, and proceed to the turn without over-committing to a potentially dangerous pot.

The turn card completes the club flush, and my King-high with no flush draw has zero equity. Since Flashlight checked back the flop, they might have some marginal showdown value, a club draw that just got there, or they might just bet if checked to. Betting out as a pure bluff into a flush-completing board with no blockers is highly unprofitable. Checking and preparing to fold to any bet is the only sensible choice.

The board texture has become more coordinated with the turn 4c, completing a potential flush draw. My opponent (Pro) has checked twice, which could indicate a trap or simply that they missed their range. Betting now might alienate weaker hands I could potentially get more value from on the river or bloat the pot against a trap. With top pair, I have decent showdown value and checking behind keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize my equity, and keeps my opponent in the hand for a potential river bet if they choose to play a draw-heavy or polarized line. Checking is the most balanced and cautious approach.

The river brings the 6d, completing a 7d 8c Qc 4c 6d board. Having checked both the flop and turn, my line looks like given-up overcards or a marginal pair. My opponent checking back twice suggests they likely have marginal showdown value such as Ace-high, a weak pair, or a hand they are cautiously pot-controlling. With Kh9h and no club blockers, bluffing into this board is highly risky and likely unprofitable, as I don't credibly represent a flush and my opponent will often call with their bluff-catchers. Checking to surrender the pot is the safest and most standard GTO approach.

The board is coordinated with straight and flush possibilities. My opponent checked the river, which looks like an attempt to check-call or a surrender with a missed draw (like Ace-high flush draws). Betting with top pair might only get called by hands that beat me (like a slow-played set or two pair) or get check-raised, while folding out hands that I beat. Checking back allows me to take my showdown value and avoids the risk of running into a check-raise or overvaluing my hand against a trap.
Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand. Being on the button does give us position, but this hand has very low equity and playability post-flop. The standard and optimal play is to fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. It's a three-handed game, and the button has folded, leaving the action between me and Flashlight in the Big Blind. Ace-high is a strong hand in this situation, but A2o plays relatively poorly out of position post-flop. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and represent strength, rather than just calling the 50 and playing a small pot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-2 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. While the pot odds are 2:1, the hand's potential to win against a raising range is very low, even heads-up. It is disciplined to fold here.

I am on the button with 3s 8c. This is a very weak hand. While I have a healthy stack and being on the button often warrants an open, 83o is near the bottom of my range. My opponents, Flashlight and Pro, have been active. Given my stack size and the hand strength, folding is the standard and most prudent play here to wait for a better spot.

With Ten-Trey offsuit in the Small Blind against the Big Blind, the hand value is extremely weak and unlikely to perform well in a post-flop scenario. Given that I have already posted the small blind, folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack and avoid playing a negative EV hand out of position.

The opponent, who checked behind on the flop, has now bet 300 into a pot of 850 on the turn. My hand, Ace-high with no pair and no legitimate draw, is extremely weak. I have an overcard (King) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) on a relatively coordinated board (9h-Js-7h). The board is extremely dangerous, with straight possibilities (6-high and T-high) and a completed flush draw on the river. My pair of Nines is a very marginal holding on this coordinated board. I have the bottom end of the straight (5-6-7-8-9) on a board with three hearts and a higher straight possibility for any Ten.
